title: St. Athanasius (Monthly) — St. Acacius of Byzantium & St. Tarachus
narrative:
St. Athanasius the Apostolic, Archbishop of Alexandria and Pillar of Orthodoxy, St. Acacius of Byzantium the Bishop-Martyr, and St. Tarachus the Soldier-Martyr

On the seventh of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy, glorious, and God-bearing Athanasius the Apostolic, Archbishop of Alexandria, the man who more than any other single figure preserved the Nicene faith against the Arian heresy through five exiles and nearly two decades spent outside his see — the bishop whose life became a living embodiment of the principle that the Church is governed not by imperial power but by divine truth. Athanasius is commemorated monthly in the Ethiopian calendar because his theological legacy is not peripheral but constitutive: the Nicene formula that he defended with his life — that the Son is homoousios, of one substance with the Father — is the foundation upon which all Orthodox Christology rests, and without it the Incarnation would be merely a visitation of a divine creature rather than the self-giving of the eternal God. Every Eucharist the Ethiopian church celebrates is a celebration that Athanasius made possible.

The particular gift of Athanasius to the Ethiopian tradition goes beyond his Trinitarian theology. He was the bishop who wrote the thirty-ninth Festal Letter that first defined the canonical list of twenty-seven books of the New Testament — a list the Ethiopian church received and, in its characteristic way, both preserved and expanded with its own deuterocanonical additions. He was the bishop who recognised the young Anthony of the desert as the model of Christian life and wrote his Life, thereby establishing the hagiographical genre that would structure all subsequent Christian biography. And he was the bishop who understood, with a clarity rare in any age, that the struggle over the word homoousios was not an academic dispute but a question about the salvation of humanity: if the Son is merely similar to the Father and not one with him, then God has not truly entered human flesh, and the Incarnation offers no genuine transformation — only a divine visitation that leaves the human condition fundamentally unchanged.

Saint Acacius of Byzantium is the great discovery of this day: a bishop of the imperial capital who stands in the tradition's memory at the intersection of theology and imperial politics in a way that illuminates Athanasius's own struggle from a different angle. Acacius was a bishop who navigated the complex currents of the late fourth century with less consistency than his contemporary heroes: he vacillated between Nicene and semi-Arian positions under varying imperial pressures, and his name is associated with what later theologians would call the Acacian Schism. Yet the tradition retains his feast because his martyrdom — which came at last when he refused a demand that exceeded even his considerable flexibility — demonstrated that the capacity for costly fidelity exists even in those whose earlier record has been compromised. His death is the tradition's statement that God is not done with those whose courage has faltered: the final confession can redeem the earlier hesitations, and the martyr's crown waits for those who did not receive it at the first opportunity.

Saint Tarachus is among the most celebrated of the soldier-martyrs whose feast falls in Ginbot, and his story contains a detail that has made him beloved in the tradition: he was an elderly soldier, already retired from military service, who was arrested during the Diocletianic persecution and subjected to tortures that the tradition records with particular attention to their repeated failure. Again and again the instruments of torture produced their expected effects on younger men but seemed to exhaust themselves on Tarachus, who endured what the sources describe as an almost serene patience that unnerved his torturers more than any resistance would have. The tradition sees in his aged endurance the consummation of the military virtue of Athanasius's long patience: as Athanasius endured five exiles with a steadiness his contemporaries found incomprehensible, so Tarachus endured the arena with a steadiness his torturers could not explain. Both are witnesses to the grace that does not diminish with the years but deepens into something that the world, with all its instruments, cannot overcome. Amen.
